ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef bowl entry; no specific kimchi gyudon entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef dish, mixed preparation, with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated kimchi gyudon code. A0FJP (beef dish with rice) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for kimchi gyudon or gyudon as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g (one bowl), providing roughly 519 kcal, 24.2 g protein, 17.3 g fat, and 62.3 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled; White Rice, cooked; Kimchi, commercially prepared
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kimchi Gyudon is a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 168878), cooked thinly sliced beef (FDC ID 174032), and commercially prepared kimchi (FDC ID 169975), weighted by approximate dish composition (~55% rice, ~30% beef, ~15% kimchi by weight). The input record values (energy 115.38 kcal, protein 5.38 g, fat 3.85 g, carbohydrate 13.85 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composition estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 276.9 mg/100 g is plausible given soy sauce and kimchi contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for kimchi gyudon as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: short-grain Japanese white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The beef and kimchi components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 62.3 g) / 100 = 44.9 g GL per 450 g bowl.

hypertension.key_factor: High cumulative sodium from soy sauce, mirin, and kimchi; estimated 276.9 mg sodium per 100 g equates to approximately 1,246 mg per 450 g serving, approaching 54% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and limit kimchi quantity, as a single 450 g bowl may contribute over 1,200 mg sodium; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Short-grain Japanese white rice has a GI of approximately 72 and the dish delivers approximately 62.3 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving with only 3.5 g dietary fibre, producing a high glycemic load of ~44.9 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute short-grain white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycemic index significantly, and request reduced-sugar preparation to lower the glycemic load of the simmering sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.54 g/100 g (6.93 g per 450 g serving) from beef and trans fat at 0.08 g/100 g, combined with high sodium, present a moderate cardiovascular risk profile; cholesterol at 27.69 mg/100 g (124.6 mg per serving) is within acceptable range.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, consider substituting beef with a leaner protein such as chicken breast or tofu, and request preparation without added sugar in the simmering sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Thinly sliced beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies inflammatory prostaglandin pathways relevant to RA; kimchi provides probiotic benefit but does not offset the pro-inflammatory red meat component.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with chicken, tofu, or mushrooms, while retaining the kimchi component for its probiotic and gut-immune modulation benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Gyudon (キムチ牛丼) is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish consisting of thinly sliced beef and onions simmered in a savoury-sweet sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and dashi stock, served over a bed of steamed short-grain Japanese white rice and topped with spicy fermented kimchi. The dish combines the umami richness of classic Japanese gyudon with the tangy, spicy, and probiotic character of Korean kimchi. The texture profile includes tender simmered beef, soft sticky rice, and crunchy-fermented kimchi. It is commonly served in a single bowl format (approximately 450 g) and is popular in Singapore's Japanese fast-food restaurant chains. Nutritionally, a 450 g serving provides approximately 519 kcal, 24.2 g protein, 17.3 g fat, and 62.3 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1,246 mg per serving) due to soy sauce and kimchi, and has a high glycemic load (~44.9) driven by short-grain white rice. It is not suitable for patients with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or those managing cardiovascular risk without dietary modification. Kimchi contributes probiotic Lactobacillus species, vitamin C (~6.9 mg per serving), and modest iron (~2.8 mg per serving).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a kimchi beef bowl?
Is kimchi gyudon suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of kimchi gyudon?
What is gyudon topped with kimchi called?
キムチ牛丼 カロリー

confidence_reason: Kimchi Gyudon is a composite fusion dish not directly listed in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked short-grain white rice, cooked beef, and commercially prepared kimchi, weighted by approximate dish composition. The input record values are consistent with this estimate and are retained. GI is assigned by analogy to short-grain Japanese white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite kimchi gyudon dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Values would require laboratory analysis of the specific dish formulation. Two portions are noted in the input: 1 bowl (450 g, default) and 1 set meal (650 g). The 450 g bowl is used as the primary serving reference. The set meal portion (650 g) includes additional items (egg, soup/drink) not reflected in the per-100g nutrient profile. Halal status is not confirmed as mirin and sake are alcohol-containing ingredients; the dish is not considered halal in standard preparation. The input record had two portions both flagged is_default: true, which is inconsistent; the 450 g bowl is treated as the primary default serving.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements.
Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Korean to local_names and aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 for a dish adapted in Singapore.
Ensured all new local names were added to the flat 'aliases' array for consistency (q8 self-check).
